full_response:
The given invariant distance measure is \(\Delta s^2 = -\Delta t^2 + \alpha^2 t^2 \Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2 + \Delta z^2\), which corresponds to the line element \(ds^2 = -dt^2 + \alpha^2 t^2 dx^2 + dy^2 + dz^2\). This metric is related to the standard Minkowski metric via a coordinate transformation. Consider the transformation:

\[
T = t \cosh(\alpha x), \quad X = t \sinh(\alpha x), \quad Y = y, \quad Z = z.
\]

Computing the differentials:

\[
\begin{aligned}
dT &= \cosh(\alpha x) \, dt + \alpha t \sinh(\alpha x) \, dx, \\
dX &= \sinh(\alpha x) \, dt + \alpha t \cosh(\alpha x) \, dx.
\end{aligned}
\]

Then,

\[
\begin{aligned}
-dT^2 + dX^2 &= -\left[ \cosh^2(\alpha x) \, dt^2 + 2\alpha t \cosh(\alpha x) \sinh(\alpha x) \, dt \, dx + \alpha^2 t^2 \sinh^2(\alpha x) \, dx^2 \right] \\
&\quad + \left[ \sinh^2(\alpha x) \, dt^2 + 2\alpha t \sinh(\alpha x) \cosh(\alpha x) \, dt \, dx + \alpha^2 t^2 \cosh^2(\alpha x) \, dx^2 \right] \\
&= (-\cosh^2 + \sinh^2) \, dt^2 + 0 \cdot dt \, dx + \alpha^2 t^2 (\cosh^2 - \sinh^2) \, dx^2 \\
&= -dt^2 + \alpha^2 t^2 \, dx^2.
\end{aligned}
\]

Adding \(dY^2 + dZ^2 = dy^2 + dz^2\) yields the original line element. Thus, the transformed metric is \(ds^2 = -dT^2 + dX^2 + dY^2 + dZ^2\), the standard Minkowski metric. This shows the spacetime is flat, and any invariant quantities (e.g., curvature, geodesics) computed in parts (a) and (b) must align with those of Minkowski space. For instance, if part (a) computed the Riemann tensor, it would vanish, and if part (b) derived geodesics, they would be straight lines in
